title: Why America is Blindly Walking Into a Trap in Iran - Prof. Jiang Xueqin
full_text: Every empire carries its own assassin inside it. The underdog's job is simply to recognize the weakness first. That is the law most people miss when they look at war. They think war is about size. Who has more money? Who has more missiles? Who has more aircraft? Who has more men? Who has more allies? Who has more factories? Who has more time? And once they count those things, they think they have already counted victory. That is how most people read empires. They see scale and they mistake scale for destiny. But history is not that obedient. History has a darker habit. Again and again, it lets the giant grow so large, so wealthy, and so certain of itself that its very strengths begin turning against it. Its mass becomes social decay. Its organization becomes elite corruption. Its depth becomes arrogance. And by the time the empire still looks too big to fail, the underdog has often already found the crack. That is the secret. Weak countries do not defeat empires by becoming bigger than the empire. They defeat empires by becoming more alive than the empire, more motivated, more adaptive, more united. That is the law of asymmetry. And if you understand that law, then you understand why I believe a smaller power can survive, resist, and even defeat a much larger one. Now, let us begin very slowly because this is the kind of idea that changes how you see all of history once you really understand it. The first thing you need to understand is that empires do have real advantages. I am not denying that. A serious theory of war begins with reality, not romance. And the reality is that an empire usually has three enormous strengths. First, mass. Second, organization. Third, depth. These are not minor things. These are overwhelming advantages. Mass means population, territory, labor, resources, and the ability to draw on huge networks of support. An empire is never just a country in the narrow sense. It is a structure. It can pull from allies, client states, dependent regions, intelligence networks, supply systems, overseas bases, industrial partnerships, and international financial power. When a true empire fights, it rarely fights alone. Even when its own flag seems to be at the center, there are layers of human and material support behind it. That is mass. And mass matters because mass gives redundancy. Mass means that losses do not immediately break you. Mass means you can lose one campaign and still return. Mass means that even failure can be absorbed. Now the second strength is organization. This is what turns scale into power. Organization means bureaucracy, command structure, finance, science, engineering, logistics, elite coordination, intelligence management, and the ability to process huge amounts of information and force. Without organization, large population is just a burden. With organization, it becomes a machine. That machine can build bombers, satellites, missile systems, naval fleets, cyber capabilities, surveillance networks, precision targeting, industrial supply chains, and all the terrifying instruments of modern war. That is why organization is such a formidable imperial advantage. It transforms chaos into capacity. And the third strength is depth. Depth means resilience. It means the empire can afford mistakes. It can lose money and raise more. It can lose equipment and replace more. It can lose one war and still remain globally significant. It has room. It has margin. It has layers of protection between defeat in one theater and collapse of the whole system. That is why empires often appear immortal because depth gives the impression that no single blow can truly kill them. Now if these three strengths mass, organization and depth worked forever in the same direction, then empire would be eternal. That would be the end of history. The biggest system would win and once it won, it would stay on top forever. But that is not what happens. That is not what has ever happened. Empires rise, they expand, they dominate, and then they rot, then they fracture, then they make mistakes so stupid that future generations can hardly believe such a large power made them. That is the pattern. So if empires still fall despite mass, despite organization, despite depth, then something else must be true. The truth is this. The same advantages that make an empire strong in the beginning become poisonous over time. That is the real theory. The empire is not destroyed in spite of its strengths. It is often destroyed because its strengths slowly mutate into weaknesses. Now let us look at mass first. Mass looks glorious from the outside. More people, more workers, more soldiers, more taxpayers, more consumers, more reach. But over time, mass produces another reality. Competition, overcrowding, inequality, distance between rulers and ruled. A giant system begins concentrating wealth upward and pressure downward. The more successful the empire becomes, the more complex the internal struggle for position becomes. And eventually, mass does not produce vitality. It produces exhaustion. Ordinary people become overworked, indebted, cynical and disconnected from whatever grand imperial mission is being announced from the capital. That is one of the first hidden weaknesses of empire. The population becomes large but not spiritually alive. Big but not motivated, rich in aggregate but hollow in morale. The empire still has bodies but not necessarily belief. And belief matters more in war than comfortable societies usually remember. Now let us go to organization. This is where the rot becomes even more dangerous. In the beginning, organization is efficient. It rewards competence. It concentrates talent. It creates structure. But over time, the elite who manage the structure begin serving themselves. This is one of the oldest cycles in political history. The people closest to the machinery of power stop asking how to strengthen the hole and start asking how to monetize their position inside the hole. That is when organization becomes extraction. The elite become parasitic. Institutions begin reproducing themselves instead of solving real problems. And because empire is wealthy, many people begin wanting entry into the elite itself. That creates another disease. Too many ambitious people competing for a limited number of high status positions. This is what some historians call elite overprouction. A society starts generating more claimants to influence than the system can satisfy. The result is factionalism, infighting, polarization, court politics, bureaucratic stagnation. The empire becomes less interested in reality and more interested in itself. That is a fatal stage because a state obsessed with internal elite warfare becomes strategically blind. It stops seeing the world clearly. It starts interpreting external conflict through domestic factional advantage. Now you still have the outward appearance of organization. The paperwork still exists. The chains of command still exist. The ministry still exist. The military still exists. But the soul of the structure is changing. The organization is no longer a disciplined instrument. It is becoming a battlefield of competing parasites. That is why giant powers can look so sophisticated and yet make such embarrassing mistakes. Their systems are still large but no longer clean. Now let us go to depth. Depth is the most psychologically dangerous advantage of all. Because if you can afford failure, then failure stops teaching you properly. That is the curse. A small state that fails may die. So a small state must learn. A great empire can fail and survive. And because it survives, it often learns the wrong lesson. It tells itself the failure did not matter. It tells itself it can correct later. It tells itself scale will protect it. That is how depth becomes hubris. Hubris is not just arrogance in the ordinary sense. It is blindness created by previous success. It is the inability to feel consequences at the right time. It is the habit of repeating bad judgment because the cost always seems survivable until one day it is not. That is why great powers often become strategically lazy, not materially lazy, intellectually lazy. They begin assuming that because they still possess immense force, their judgment must still be sound. That is one of the most dangerous illusions in international politics. The empire keeps making mistakes and keeps surviving them. And survival itself becomes fuel for more mistakes. This is how a giant becomes stupid. Not all at once, gradually, confidently. Now put all three together. Mass becomes inequality, fatigue, and apathy. Organization becomes elite competition, corruption, and factionalism. Depth becomes arrogance, complacency, and repeated miscalculation. That is the hidden architecture of imperial decline. And now we arrive at the crucial point. An empire can carry all of that internal weakness and still look terrifying from the outside. That is why people misread history. They see the weapons but not the rot. They see the aircraft carriers but not the internal exhaustion. They see the money but not the corruption of decision-making. They see the empire's scale and miss the fact that the system is no longer metabolizing reality properly. And that is where the underdog enters the story. Now, let us be very clear. The underdog does not win by becoming an empire faster than the empire. That would be absurd. The underdog wins through asymmetry. The underdog wins by cultivating the opposite qualities. Where the empire is exhausted, the underdog becomes energetic. Where the empire is closed and arrogant, the underdog becomes open and adaptive. Where the empire is fractured, the underdog becomes cohesive. That is the whole secret. Not bigger, sharper. Now let us take the first quality, energy. Energy is one of the most underestimated forces in war. People think of war in terms of steel and money. They should think more in terms of life. Energy means the willingness to struggle seriously, to endure, to improvise, to absorb punishment without surrendering the core sense of purpose. Empires often lose this quality because life inside the imperial center becomes too comfortable, too distracted, too fragmented, too cynical. The underdog, by contrast, often experiences war as immediate reality. Not a policy discussion, not a cable news topic, not a partisan talking point, but a question of survival. And survival creates seriousness. That seriousness becomes a form of power. A society that truly feels the war in its bones can become much more dangerous than a richer society that is only half present psychologically. That is why underdogs often fight with a kind of intensity that empires cannot manufacture on command. Now the second quality, openness. This is extremely important. Openness means the ability to learn, to adapt, to admit mistakes, to promote useful talent instead of merely rewarded status. Empires become rigid because their institutions get too invested in preserving established patterns. An empire often keeps doing what worked before, even after reality has changed. The underdog does not have that luxury. If the underdog fails to learn, it dies. That is one of the greatest strategic gifts of weakness. Weakness forces realism. A smaller state under extreme pressure becomes far more likely to innovate tactically, politically, and socially because it must. This is why underdogs can become surprisingly resilient. They are under evolutionary pressure. Every mistake matters. Every lesson matters. Every adaptation matters. While the empire is often repeating formulas, the underdog is being forced into invention. That is a tremendous advantage. Now, the third quality, cohesion. This may be the most decisive one because war is not only about weapons. It is about whether a society can align itself around sacrifice. An empire can be large and still be internally broken. A smaller power can be much more dangerous if it is united by a clear purpose. Cohesion turns pain into endurance. Cohesion turns fear into discipline. Cohesion turns a scattered population into a historical actor. This does not mean perfect harmony. No society is perfect. But relative cohesion matters enormously. If the underdog feels united while the empire feels inwardly at war with itself, then the strategic balance begins changing underneath the surface. This is why the underdog can look weak to the outside world while actually becoming stronger in the dimensions that decide long wars. Now apply this to the United States and Iran. The United States is the empire in this framework. Not because it is evil in some cartoon sense, but because it has the structure, mass, organization, depth. It has enormous alliances, advanced military infrastructure, financial scale, institutional complexity, and the ability to absorb shocking amounts of cost. All true, but it also carries the classic diseases of late imperial power. internal inequality, polarization, elite conflict, bureaucratic rigidity, strategic arrogance, a political class often more obsessed with itself than with reality. That matters because those are not side issues. Those are war variables. Now ask the real question. Can Iran become more energetic than the empire? Can it become more open to adaptation than the empire? Can it become more cohesive than the empire? That is the entire issue. Because if the answer is yes, then raw scale becomes less decisive than people assume. Now let us slow down and think carefully. Can Iran become energetic? That does not mean can it shout louder. It means can its society and institutions become more serious under pressure? Can war harden it instead of hollowing it out? Can the danger focus it? Can suffering become fuel instead of paralysis? If yes, then the underdog is already gaining one of the most important asymmetrical advantages. Now openness. Can Iran learn faster? Can it adapt faster? Can it recognize errors and innovate under stress? That matters because empires often keep doing more of what once worked while underdogs are forced to discover what works now. If the underdog becomes more adaptive, then every month of the war may strengthen its strategic competence while the empire remains trapped in formulas. That is how seemingly weaker systems become dangerous. Now, cohesion. Can Iran stay together politically and socially under pressure? Can it create enough unity of purpose to avoid internal fragmentation during conflict? Because if it can, then it will possess something the empire may no longer be able to summon in equal measure. And if one side is truly cohesive while the other is internally corroded, then the psychological structure of the war shifts dramatically. That is why I keep saying people focus on the wrong metrics. They count bombs, they count budgets, they count aircraft, they count reserves, but they often fail to count vitality. And vitality is what wins long historical struggles more often than people think. Now, let me make something very clear. This is not romantic underdog mythology. Weak states lose all the time. Many underdogs are crushed. Many never become energetic, open, or cohesive. Many simply fragment under pressure. So, asymmetry is not a guarantee. It is an opportunity structure. The empire carries the seeds of decay. The underdog carries the possibility of transformation. The question is whether that transformation actually happens. That is the decisive question. If the underdog stays corrupt, stagnant, divided and unmotivated, then empire scale may still crush it. But if the underdog transforms while the empire stagnates, then history begins bending in a very different direction. That is when the impossible starts becoming possible. Now, this is why I say do not become hypnotized by imperial technology. Technology matters of course but technology does not fight by itself. It is operated inside political and moral systems. If the operators are complacent, divided, arrogant and strategically rigid, then technology cannot permanently compensate for that. Similarly, do not become hypnotized by GDP. GDP does not automatically produce social endurance. Do not become hypnotized by alliance size. Alliances do not automatically produce internal cohesion. Those things matter but they are not the whole war. The deeper war is in the structure of the societies themselves. That is where asymmetry lives. Now let us push to the final insight. The empire does not usually reform itself fast enough. That is the tragedy. By the time the diseases of empire have matured, the system is often too heavy, too bureaucratic, too self-protective, and too arrogant to correct itself honestly. It keeps mistaking mass for legitimacy, bureaucracy for intelligence, scale for vitality. The underdog, by contrast, has no such luxury. It either transforms or it dies. And that is why the underdog can become more historically dangerous than the empire expects. Because pressure is making it sharper while comfort has made the empire duller. That is the true meaning of asymmetry, not equality of power, opposition of structures. The giant is large but decaying. The smaller society is vulnerable but capable of renewal. And if renewal beats decay, then the weaker side may prevail. Now, let me end by bringing everything together. Why do weak countries sometimes beat empires? Because empire strengths are temporary. Mass becomes exhaustion and inequality. Organization becomes elite corruption and factionalism. Depth becomes arrogance and repeated misjudgment. Against that, the underdog has a path. It must become more energetic than the empire, more open than the empire, more cohesive than the empire. If it does that, then it does not need to outspend the empire. It does not need to outbuild the empire. It does not need to become another empire. It only needs to become something the empire no longer knows how to be alive, adaptive, and united. That is why every empire carries its own assassin inside it. Because the empire creates the conditions of its own stupidity. And the underdog wins when it learns how to strike precisely at that weakness. So the next time someone tells you the bigger side must obviously win, ask a more serious question. Not who is bigger, who is still alive as a system? Because that is the real battlefield. And that is why history so often betrays the giant.

tragedy. By the time the diseases of
empire have matured, the system is often
too heavy, too bureaucratic, too
self-protective, and too arrogant to
correct itself honestly. It keeps
mistaking mass for legitimacy,
bureaucracy for intelligence, scale for
vitality. The underdog, by contrast, has
no such luxury. It either transforms or
it dies. And that is why the underdog
can become more historically dangerous
than the empire expects. Because
pressure is making it sharper while
comfort has made the empire duller. That
is the true meaning of asymmetry, not
equality of power, opposition of
structures. The giant is large but
decaying. The smaller society is
vulnerable but capable of renewal. And
if renewal beats decay, then the weaker
side may prevail. Now, let me end by
bringing everything together. Why do
weak countries sometimes beat empires?
Because empire strengths are temporary.
Mass becomes exhaustion and inequality.
Organization becomes elite corruption
and factionalism. Depth becomes
arrogance and repeated misjudgment.
Against that, the underdog has a path.
It must become more energetic than the
empire, more open than the empire, more
cohesive than the empire. If it does
that, then it does not need to outspend
the empire. It does not need to outbuild
the empire. It does not need to become
another empire. It only needs to become
something the empire no longer knows how
to be alive, adaptive, and united. That
is why every empire carries its own
assassin inside it. Because the empire
creates the conditions of its own
stupidity. And the underdog wins when it
learns how to strike precisely at that
weakness. So the next time someone tells
you the bigger side must obviously win,
ask a more serious question. Not who is
bigger, who is still alive as a system?
Because that is the real battlefield.
And that is why history so often betrays
the giant.
